title: NBTs Explained: What to Expect From South Africa's National Benchmark Tests
author: Tklesson Team
content:
Your National Senior Certificate results are not the only factor in South African university admission. Many universities also require you to write the National Benchmark Tests, or NBTs. These tests assess your academic readiness for university-level work in areas that the NSC does not fully measure. The NBTs are not a pass or fail examination, but your results can affect whether you are admitted to your chosen course, whether you are placed in an extended degree program, or whether you receive additional academic support. Here is what the NBTs actually test and how to prepare for them.

What the NBTs Actually Assess

The National Benchmark Tests are designed by Higher Education South Africa and are used by many South African universities as part of their admission and placement processes. The tests assess three domains. The Academic Literacy test measures your ability to read and understand academic texts, to interpret information, and to reason with written material. The Quantitative Literacy test measures your ability to apply mathematical reasoning to real-world problems. The Mathematics test, which is taken only by students applying to courses that require Mathematics, measures your proficiency in the core mathematical concepts from the school curriculum.

The tests are not based on the NSC syllabus. You cannot study for them by reviewing your school notes. They test underlying skills that develop over years of education: reading comprehension, logical reasoning, numerical fluency, and the ability to interpret data. A student who has built strong foundational skills through consistent academic engagement will perform well. A student who relied on memorization and cramming may find the NBTs challenging, regardless of their NSC marks.

The NBT results are reported as proficiency levels: proficient, intermediate, or basic. A proficient result means you are well-prepared for university study in that domain. An intermediate result means you may need some additional support. A basic result means you are likely to struggle without significant intervention. Universities use these results alongside your NSC results to make admission and placement decisions. A student with strong NSC results but weak NBT results may be offered a place in an extended degree program rather than the standard program. A student with moderate NSC results but strong NBT results may strengthen their case for admission.

How the Academic Literacy Test Works

The Academic Literacy test presents you with passages of academic writing and asks you to answer questions about them. The passages are drawn from a range of disciplines and are typical of the kind of reading you will encounter at university. The questions test your ability to understand the main idea, to make inferences, to evaluate arguments, to understand vocabulary in context, and to recognize the author's purpose and tone.

You cannot prepare for this test by memorizing content. You prepare by reading regularly. Read newspapers. Read magazines. Read books. The more you read, the more comfortable you become with the kind of complex, academic writing that the test presents. Practice summarising what you read. After reading an article, write a few sentences capturing the main argument and the key evidence. Practice distinguishing between fact and opinion. Practice identifying the author's perspective. These skills are what the Academic Literacy test measures.

How the Quantitative Literacy Test Works

The Quantitative Literacy test presents you with numerical information in real-world contexts and asks you to reason with it. You might be shown a graph, a table, or a set of statistics and asked to interpret the data, draw conclusions, or make predictions. You might be asked to calculate percentages, ratios, or rates. The mathematics itself is not advanced. The challenge is applying mathematical reasoning to unfamiliar situations.

Prepare by practicing data interpretation. When you encounter a graph or a table in a newspaper or online, study it. Ask yourself what it shows. What are the axes? What is the scale? What trends are visible? What conclusions can you draw? Practice estimating and checking the reasonableness of your estimates. The Quantitative Literacy test rewards the ability to think numerically, not the ability to perform complex calculations.

How the Mathematics Test Works

The Mathematics test is taken only by students applying to courses that require Mathematics. It tests your understanding of core mathematical concepts: algebra, functions, trigonometry, geometry, and data handling. The questions are designed to assess whether you have a genuine understanding of mathematical principles, not just the ability to follow procedures. A question might ask you to explain why a particular approach works, or to apply a concept in an unfamiliar context.

Prepare by reviewing the foundational concepts from Grades 10, 11, and 12. Practice solving problems that require you to explain your reasoning. The Mathematics test rewards understanding, not just correct answers. A student who can explain why a formula works will perform better than a student who can only apply it mechanically.

The Bottom Line

South Africa's National Benchmark Tests assess academic readiness in ways that the NSC does not fully capture. The Academic Literacy test rewards regular reading. The Quantitative Literacy test rewards comfort with numerical reasoning. The Mathematics test rewards genuine conceptual understanding. Prepare over the long term by building the foundational skills these tests measure. In the weeks before the test, familiarize yourself with the format and practice with sample questions. The NBTs are not a hurdle to fear. They are a tool that helps universities place you in the right program for your current level of readiness.
